description: A requirement is verifiable if there exists a finite and cost-effective process to check whether the system meets the requirement. Avoid subjective terms like 'fast', 'easy', 'secure', 'sufficient'.

criterion.0: The requirement uses subjective or unmeasurable terms with no metric whatsoever. There is no way to test it.
criterion.1: The requirement attempts to include a metric but it is incomplete, imprecise or lacks context such as load conditions or scope.
criterion.2: The requirement includes a concrete, measurable metric with clear conditions that can be tested objectively.

description: A requirement is atomic if it expresses a single idea or function. It should not contain conjunctions that join two or more independent requirements.

criterion.0: The requirement expresses two or more independent ideas joined by conjunctions like 'and', 'or', 'also'.
criterion.1: The requirement is mostly atomic but contains a secondary idea that could be separated.
criterion.2: The requirement expresses exactly one idea and can stand alone without being split further.

description: A requirement is unambiguous if it has one and only one interpretation. Every stakeholder reading it should understand the same thing.

criterion.0: The requirement uses terms that can be interpreted in multiple ways with no further clarification.
criterion.1: The requirement is mostly clear but contains at least one term that could lead to different interpretations depending on context.
criterion.2: The requirement has exactly one possible interpretation. All terms are precise and context is clearly defined.

description: A requirement is complete if it contains all necessary information to implement and verify it: the condition under which it applies, the action the system must take, and the expected result.

criterion.0: The requirement is missing two or more of the following: condition, action, expected result.
criterion.1: The requirement includes some necessary information but is missing one key element such as the triggering condition or the expected outcome.
criterion.2: The requirement clearly states the condition, the action the system must take, and the expected result.

description: A requirement is traceable if it can be linked back to its origin such as a stakeholder need, use case or business rule, and forward to its implementation artifacts.

criterion.0: The requirement has no reference to any origin, stakeholder, use case or business rule.
criterion.1: The requirement has a partial reference to its origin but it is unclear or incomplete.
criterion.2: The requirement clearly references its origin and can be linked to a stakeholder need, use case or business rule.
